title: The Slump In Asian Currencies
text:
The Slump In Asian Currencies

Udith Sikand, Tom Miller

Gavekal Research | 30 Jun 2026

Asian trading on Tuesday saw the Japanese yen hit ¥162.40 against the US dollar, its weakest since December 1986 in the early days of the bubble economy following the signing of the Plaza Accord. This latest drop in the yen follows steep falls in recent weeks from other Asian currencies including the Korean won, Thai baht and Indonesian rupiah.It was not supposed to be like this. Steps towards peace in the Middle East and the beginning of a resumption in normal energy trade had been expected to bring relief for the currencies of Asian oil and gas importers. A preliminary deal in the Gulf, it was thought, would reverse the downward revision in the region’s growth outlook that followed the start of the war and see capital begin to flow back into the region. Instead, following an initial pop when the US and Iran first announced their memorandum of understanding, almost all Asian currencies have weakened as the US dollar has rallied on expectations that the Federal Reserve will hike rates (see A Concentrated Inflationary Boom Or A Bet On The Fed?).
